attribution: Original text authored for this fixture, 2026

paragraphs.text: The town of Vellermoor owes its existence to a flooded ferry crossing. In the spring of 1411 the river Aulne shifted its channel by nearly half a mile, stranding the old landing at Crosswick and forcing traders to unload on the eastern marsh instead. Within a generation the marsh had been drained, planked, and crowded with warehouses, and the ferrymen's chapel that once marked open water stood at the centre of a market square.

Coins of the Brennard dukes are scarce and badly struck. The mint at Hollowgate operated for barely forty years, and its die-cutters seem never to have mastered the lettering of their own motto, which appears in at least six corrupted forms. Yet the coins circulated widely: hoards containing Brennard pennies have been recorded as far south as the Tessaline coast, evidence of a trade reach quite out of proportion to the duchy's modest size.

No single document describes the Amber Causeway in its entirety, and historians have had to reconstruct the route from fragments: a toll register here, a merchant's complaint there, the itinerary of a bishop who travelled it in stages over three summers. What emerges is less a road than a habit. From the salt pans of Ostreval the track ran inland through the beech country, keeping to the gravel ridges where carts would not founder, and crossed the Aulne at whichever ford the season permitted. At Vellermoor the goods changed hands and often changed nature entirely — raw amber and salted eels going south, finished glass and dyed wool coming north — so that a merchant at one end of the causeway might have only the vaguest notion of what his goods became at the other. The toll registers of the Brennard dukes, kept with more enthusiasm than accuracy, suggest that traffic peaked in the 1460s, when as many as ninety carts a week passed the Hollowgate bar in high summer. The decline, when it came, was not dramatic. The river silted; the salt pans flooded; a new road, straighter and drier, was cut through the hills to the west. By 1520 the causeway survived mainly in lawsuits, as villages along its course sued one another over the maintenance of bridges that almost nobody crossed. Travellers in the eighteenth century reported finding stretches of it intact in the woods, the gravel still firm underfoot, leading confidently from nowhere to nowhere.

The expedition that left Port Carrowane in the autumn of 1782 was equipped for a survey of six months and an absence of twelve; it returned, what remained of it, after three years and four months. Its commander, a methodical and unimaginative man whose journals are the chief source for everything that followed, had been instructed to chart the inlets of the southern archipelago and to make note of any timber suitable for masts. The first winter accounted for one ship, driven onto the Harrow Shoals in a gale the pilots had been assured was out of season. The second winter accounted for much of the expedition's discipline. The journals grow terse at this point, recording rations, soundings, and burials in the same flat hand, and historians have argued ever since about what the commander chose not to write. What is certain is that the survivors wintered ashore at a place they named Patience Cove, that they traded with people whose name for the place they never learned to pronounce, and that the charts they brought home were so accurate that they remained in use, with minor corrections, for over a century. The commander was given a medal, a pension, and a polite hint that he should not seek another command. His journals were deposited in the Admiralty library at Carrowane, where they were catalogued, mislaid, recatalogued under a wrong name, and finally rediscovered in 1911 behind a cabinet of signal flags.

The weavers' guild of Ostreval kept two sets of books, and the discrepancy between them is the best evidence we have for how the cloth trade actually worked. The official ledger, presented annually to the duke's auditors, records steady output, fair prices, and dues paid in full. The private ledger, discovered sewn into the binding of a psalter in 1893, records the bribes, the short-weighted bales, the apprentices hired off the books, and the substantial annual payment listed only as "for the quiet of the bridge" — understood by most scholars to mean the toll-keeper, and by a stubborn minority to mean something else entirely.

The siege of Fenhallow Keep lasted eleven days and is remembered chiefly for how it ended. The garrison, down to brackish water and the last of the winter cheese, hung out signals of surrender on the twelfth morning, only to find the besieging camp empty. The opposing army had marched away in the night, summoned home by a succession crisis its commanders judged more urgent than a fen-country castle. Both sides afterwards claimed the affair as a victory, and both kept the anniversary, which fell, awkwardly, on the same feast day.

Students often assume that the salt tax of the middle Brennard period was a single, coherent institution. It was not. It was a sediment of improvisations: a war levy never repealed, a bridge toll extended to cover salt "for convenience," a ducal monopoly sold to a consortium and bought back at a loss, and a measuring standard — the Hollowgate bushel — that varied detectably from one weighing house to the next. Contemporary complaints survive in quantity, and they make the essential point better than any modern summary: nobody, including the collectors, fully understood what was owed, and the gap between theory and collection was where most of the politics of the duchy actually happened.

Annals of the port of Carrowane, in the entry for the year of the long fog: "This year the fog stood on the water from the feast of the candles until past midsummer, so that ships came in by bell and shouting. The herring failed. The pilots doubled their fees and were not loved for it. A whale came into the inner harbour and could not find its way out again for nine days, and the children fed it bread, which it did not want. In this year also the new quay was finished, two years late, and the harbourmaster's house burned down, from no cause that could be discovered, on the night of its blessing."

Excavations at the deserted village of Crosswick, conducted intermittently between 1958 and 1974, overturned most of what the documentary record had suggested. The village was larger than its tax assessments implied, better built, and abandoned later. The excavators found house platforms with glazed pottery in their final occupation layers, a smithy that had been carefully dismantled rather than left to ruin, and, beneath the chapel floor, a sequence of earlier timber churches going back further than any written mention of the place. Crosswick, it turned out, had not faded; it had moved, deliberately and almost in a body, to the new river crossing at Vellermoor — taking its roof timbers, its bell, and very nearly its entire archaeology with it.

The dynasty that historians call the House of Maren ruled its scatter of hill valleys for six generations and left almost nothing behind on purpose. No chronicle was kept at their court; no tombs were raised beyond plain slabs; the family's one surviving charter grants a mill to a monastery and misspells the duke's own name. What we know of them comes sideways — from the account books of their creditors, from the marriage complaints of their neighbours, and from a single remarkable letter in which a Maren daughter, writing to her sister, describes the family castle as "cold in the particular way that only pride keeps a house cold," and asks, in a postscript that has launched a dozen speculative articles, whether the road south was yet safe to travel in winter.
